title: Edict of Milan legalises Christianity
summary: In February 313 CE the western emperor Constantine and the eastern emperor Licinius met at the imperial residence of Mediolanum (modern Milan) and agreed a joint policy of religious toleration that ended official Roman persecution of Christians and ordered the restitution of confiscated Christian property. The agreement, promulgated in Licinius's rescript to the provincial governors of the east on 13 June 313 and preserved in the writings of Lactantius and Eusebius, is conventionally called the Edict of Milan, though modern historians point out that it was neither, strictly speaking, an edict nor issued at Milan.

sections.heading: Background
sections.body:
Christianity had grown from a small movement of followers of a crucified Galilean teacher in the early first century into a religion of perhaps five to ten per cent of the Roman Empire's population by 300 CE. Its members refused, distinctively, to sacrifice to the gods of the state cult or to the imperial genius, and were regarded by many of their neighbours as atheists and disloyal citizens. Sporadic persecutions had followed under emperors including Nero (in 64 CE after the great fire of Rome), Domitian in the 90s, Decius in 250 to 251 (whose empire-wide requirement of a sacrifice certificate was the first systematic attempt to enforce conformity), and Valerian in 257 to 260.

The most severe wave began in February 303 CE, under the tetrarchic emperor Diocletian and his eastern colleague Galerius. In an edict issued at Nicomedia (modern Izmit in Turkey), Christian churches were ordered destroyed, sacred books surrendered and burned, and clergy imprisoned; a series of further edicts in the next year extended the requirement of pagan sacrifice to all Christians on pain of death or forced labour. Enforcement was uneven. In the western provinces of Constantius Chlorus (Britain, Gaul and Spain) the persecution was mild and quickly abandoned; in the east, and particularly in Egypt, Syria and Asia Minor under Galerius and his subordinate Maximinus Daia, it was ferocious. Christian bishops such as Peter of Alexandria were executed, and thousands died. The African Donatist controversy of the following decades traced its origins to the disputed status of those who had handed over the scriptures.

Galerius himself was the first to change course. Dying of a painful disease in April 311 CE, he issued from Serdica (modern Sofia) an edict of grudging toleration, admitting that many Christians could not be brought back to the state cults and permitting them to reopen their assemblies and pray to their god for the emperor's health. Persecution effectively ceased in Galerius's provinces after his death that same month, though his successor Maximinus Daia continued to harass Christians in Syria and Egypt.

Meanwhile the political landscape of the empire was reshaping itself. Constantius Chlorus had died at York in 306 CE; his troops proclaimed his son Constantine as Augustus. Over the next six years Constantine consolidated control of the western provinces. In October 312 CE he marched on Rome against his brother-in-law Maxentius and defeated him at the battle of the Milvian Bridge just north of the city. The Christian sources Eusebius and Lactantius, writing after the event, both record that Constantine had before the battle experienced a vision, in Eusebius's version a cross-shaped sign in the sky with the Greek words en toutoi nika (In this, conquer), and in Lactantius's version a dream commanding him to mark his soldiers' shields with the Chi-Rho monogram of Christ. Whatever the truth of the vision story, Constantine emerged from Milvian Bridge favourably disposed to Christianity.

In the winter following the battle Constantine met his eastern colleague Licinius, now sole Augustus in the Balkans after the death of Galerius, at Milan (Mediolanum). The purpose of the meeting was chiefly dynastic: Licinius was to marry Constantine's half-sister Constantia, and the two emperors were to coordinate policy against their common rival Maximinus Daia in the east. Among the measures they agreed on was a joint declaration of religious toleration.

sections.heading: What happened
sections.body:
The Milan meeting produced no single surviving document called the Edict of Milan. What has come down is Licinius's rescript to the governor of Bithynia, posted at Nicomedia on 13 June 313 CE and preserved in the original Latin by the Christian writer Lactantius in his De mortibus persecutorum, chapter 48, and in Greek translation by Eusebius of Caesarea in his Ecclesiastical History, book 10 chapter 5. Constantine issued a parallel western version that has not survived independently. The text of the rescript states that Constantine and Licinius, when they met at Milan, had decided that among the matters they saw would be to the profit of the many, those of the reverence of the divinity should be arranged first, so that they might grant both to Christians and to all others the free power to follow the religion each preferred, so that whatever divinity was in the seat of the heavens might be propitious and favourable to them and to all who were placed under their authority.

The provisions were sweeping. Christians were freely to worship, without restriction. The specific status previously granted to Christians in earlier edicts was extended: all religious groups had the same freedom. All confiscated Christian property, both individual and corporate (that is, the churches themselves and their meeting places), was to be returned without delay and without payment, and current owners who lost property under this measure could apply to the imperial treasury for compensation. Above all the state undertook not merely to permit but to actively assist in restoring the church's material foundations.

This was, in a strict sense, a new departure. Galerius's edict of 311 CE had granted a bare permission to Christians to reopen their assemblies, but had not restored their property. The Milan agreement did. It also, crucially, extended religious freedom to non-Christians, framing the concession as a general policy of religious toleration rather than a special exception for one sect.

Enforcement in Licinius's territories was rapid; his rescript, addressed to the eastern governors, made the restitution effective as summer 313. In the extreme east, Maximinus Daia had for some months been rescinding parts of his earlier persecuting legislation, evidently in anticipation of the political isolation Milan portended. He now attempted a military reckoning with Licinius. On 30 April 313 CE the two eastern armies met at Tzirallum in Thrace, and Licinius, though outnumbered, defeated Maximinus, who fled and died within months. Licinius entered Nicomedia in June and posted the Milan rescript there.

How to describe the resulting document was, from the fourth century, a matter of Christian historiography rather than of Roman constitutional law. The word edictum in Roman usage referred to a formal decree of the emperor with immediate force of law; what emerged from Milan was a policy agreement, promulgated in the eastern half of the empire by an imperial rescript (a letter to an official). Modern historians, from the German scholar Otto Seeck in the nineteenth century to more recent work, have made the point that no imperial document called the Edict of Milan ever existed. The traditional title, established by early modern church historians, reflects the moment of decision rather than the technical form of the resulting law.

sections.heading: Aftermath & consequences
sections.body:
The immediate consequences varied by region. In the western provinces of Constantine, Christianity moved from a tolerated to an actively favoured religion within a decade. Constantine himself, though not baptised until his deathbed in 337, adopted the Chi-Rho as a personal and dynastic symbol, favoured Christian officials, exempted Christian clergy from municipal duties, funded church building on a monumental scale (the original Old St Peter's basilica in Rome, the Basilica of St John Lateran, the Church of the Nativity at Bethlehem and the Church of the Holy Sepulchre at Jerusalem all date to his reign), and involved himself directly in Christian doctrinal disputes. He summoned a council of North African bishops at Arles in 314 CE to adjudicate the Donatist schism and, more famously, the ecumenical council of Nicaea in 325 CE to resolve the Arian controversy over the divinity of Christ.

Relations between the two Augusti soured. War broke out between Constantine and Licinius in 316 CE, and again decisively in 324 CE, when Constantine defeated his brother-in-law at Adrianople and Chrysopolis, deposed him, and, the following year, had him executed. From 324 CE Constantine ruled the whole empire alone. He founded a new capital at the site of Byzantium on the Bosporus, dedicating it as Constantinople on 11 May 330 CE. In the new city, Christian churches and pagan temples both existed but the balance of imperial patronage was decisively Christian.

Under Constantine's sons and later successors Christianity continued to expand. A brief pagan reaction under Julian (r. 361 to 363 CE), Constantine's nephew, failed with Julian's death on campaign in Persia. In 380 CE the emperor Theodosius I, in the Edict of Thessalonica (Cunctos populos), made Nicene Christianity the state religion of the empire and, over the following years, closed pagan temples and forbade pagan sacrifice. In 391 to 392 CE further legislation formalised the end of public paganism. The Christian church, which in 300 CE had been a minority and often persecuted community, was by 400 CE the near-universal religion of the Roman state.

The practical consequences reached into every corner of Roman life: the calendar (Sunday became a rest day by Constantine's edict of 321 CE), law (Christian influence progressively softened some provisions of Roman criminal law, notably on infanticide, exposure of newborns, and gladiatorial games), architecture (the Christian basilica adapted the Roman public hall form for new liturgical purposes), and philanthropy (the earliest hospitals in the Christian sense, the xenodocheia and nosokomeia founded by bishops such as Basil of Caesarea in the later fourth century, took shape in the decades following legalisation). The empire had not been remade overnight, but the framework within which that remaking would happen was set at Milan.

sections.heading: Legacy
sections.body:
The Milan meeting is one of the most consequential turning points in the history of any religion. Its legacy is not the specific text of Licinius's rescript, which few people ever read even in antiquity, but the political reality it certified: that the Roman state had, after two and a half centuries of intermittent persecution, decided that the Christian church was not a threat to public order but a potential partner in it. Every subsequent Christian polity from Byzantine Constantinople through the medieval Latin West to Reformation and post-Reformation Europe would in some measure work out the implications of that decision.

The idea of religious toleration itself, as the rescript articulated it, would prove harder to preserve. Within a century the same imperial machinery that had legalised Christianity in 313 CE was used to suppress paganism, Judaism to a partial extent, and Christian heresies. The formula of Milan, that any person should follow the religion each preferred, was in this respect an unfulfilled promise; it would be reasserted, in different forms, by the Peace of Augsburg in 1555, the Peace of Westphalia in 1648, and the American First Amendment in 1791.

Within the Christian tradition the memory of the Milan settlement, and of Constantine as its author, has been correspondingly contested. Eastern Orthodox and Oriental Orthodox Christians revere Constantine as a saint, styling him isapostolos, equal to the apostles. Roman Catholic tradition has been more restrained but has celebrated the peace of the church that his reign inaugurated. From the Reformation onwards Protestant historians, and later some Catholic ones, have questioned whether the Constantinian settlement was an unmixed good for the church, arguing that state patronage led to worldliness, doctrinal enforcement and a loss of prophetic edge; the twentieth-century Anabaptist historian John Howard Yoder gave the argument a modern formulation.

At Milan itself the memory is honoured in the small church of San Lorenzo Maggiore, whose fourth-century core preserves the atmosphere, if not the actual walls, of the imperial city in which Constantine and Licinius met. In wider historical memory the year 313 CE has come to stand for the moment at which the ancient Mediterranean turned from one religious world to another. It is, in that respect, comparable in scale only to the conversion of India under Ashoka five and a half centuries earlier.

worldContext.text: In China the Western Jin dynasty was disintegrating in the wake of the War of the Eight Princes (291 to 306 CE), and northern China was falling to non-Han steppe peoples: Xiongnu, Xianbei, Jie, Di and Qiang, collectively the Five Barbarians. The Jin capital Luoyang had been sacked in 311 CE, and Chang'an would fall in 316 CE. In 317 CE the Jin court would re-establish itself at Jiankang (modern Nanjing) as the Eastern Jin, opening the long Northern and Southern Dynasties period. Buddhism, arrived along the Silk Road, was spreading rapidly.

In north-western and central India the last Kushan rulers were losing ground to the Sasanian Persians in the west and to rising local dynasties in the east. Within a generation, in the 320s CE, Chandragupta I would found the Gupta dynasty in Magadha, opening the classical age of Sanskrit literature, mathematics and Hindu temple architecture. Buddhist monasticism continued to flourish at Nalanda and other centres, and the great Ajanta cave paintings would in the coming century reach their peak.

In Mesoamerica the great city of Teotihuacan in the Basin of Mexico was at its height, its Pyramid of the Sun and Pyramid of the Moon dominating the Avenue of the Dead and its influence reaching south into Maya lands. In the Maya lowlands the Early Classic period was underway, with dynastic building at Tikal, Uaxactun and Copan. In the Andes the Moche culture on the north Peruvian coast was constructing the Huaca del Sol and Huaca de la Luna at its capital near modern Trujillo.

In East Africa the kingdom of Aksum, on the highlands of modern Ethiopia and Eritrea, was rising to prominence as a trading state astride the routes between the Roman world, the Nile valley and the Indian Ocean. Within a decade or two, under the king Ezana, Aksum would itself convert to Christianity through the mission of Frumentius. West Africa was seeing the growth of trans-Saharan trade networks that would in later centuries carry Islam south, and iron-working Bantu-speaking peoples continued their southward expansion into central and southern Africa.

In the western Pacific ancestral Polynesian societies in Tonga, Samoa and Fiji were consolidating the chiefly hierarchies, canoe technology and horticultural repertoire that would in later centuries carry their descendants eastward. The great voyages of settlement to the Society and Marquesas Islands were beginning or about to begin. In Australia, Aboriginal populations continued the world's oldest unbroken cultural tradition across a continent of widely varying environments.

figures.name: Constantine I (the Great)
figures.role: Roman emperor 306 to 337 CE, sole ruler from 324 CE, whose favour transformed Christianity from a persecuted minority into the imperial religion

figures.name: Licinius
figures.role: Eastern Roman Augustus 308 to 324 CE, co-signatory at Milan and issuer of the rescript that promulgated the toleration policy in the east

figures.name: Galerius
figures.role: Eastern Augustus 305 to 311 CE, principal enforcer of the Great Persecution from 303 CE and, in his last months, issuer of the earlier edict of toleration at Serdica in 311

figures.name: Maximinus Daia
figures.role: Eastern Caesar and later Augustus, holdout persecutor defeated by Licinius at Tzirallum in April 313 CE, opening the way for enforcement of the Milan policy in the east

figures.name: Lactantius
figures.role: Christian rhetorician and tutor to Constantine's son Crispus, whose De mortibus persecutorum preserves the Latin text of the Milan rescript

figures.name: Eusebius of Caesarea
figures.role: Bishop and church historian, whose Ecclesiastical History preserves the Greek translation of the rescript and the standard early Christian narrative of Constantine's rise

images.caption: Head and hand of the colossal marble statue of Constantine I, originally around twelve metres tall, from the western apse of the Basilica of Maxentius in the Roman Forum, c. 312 to 315 CE. Musei Capitolini, Rome.
images.credit: Photograph by Yair Haklai, 2009, via Wikimedia Commons

images.caption: Colossal marble head of Constantine I, fourth century CE. Museo Chiaramonti, Vatican Museums (Inv. 1749).
